topics.title: Electrical Theory & Learn
topics.intro: Understand the basics before you touch a panel. Voltage pushes, current flows, resistance slows. This is your foundation for everything else.

cards.title: Theory poster — full diagram
body: Tap the image to zoom. It walks through all nine core ideas: what electricity is, circuits, V/I/R, power, AC vs DC, home flow, series vs parallel, and wire size.

image.alt: Theory of Electricity Made Simple — full educational poster
image.caption: Theory of Electricity — Made Simple (tap to zoom)

cards.remember: Learn the picture first, then the words stick.

cards.title: What is electricity?
body: Electricity is the flow of tiny particles called electrons through a conductor (like copper wire).
Copper has free electrons. They need a complete closed loop to move.
Without a complete path, electricity does not flow.
list: They move in a closed loop
Voltage pushes them; resistance slows them
cards.remember: No loop = no flow.

cards.title: The 4 parts of every circuit
list: Return path — neutral/ground back to source
cards.remember: Source → switch → load → return. Always.

cards.title: The 4 core values (symbols)
cards.remember: V pushes, I flows, R slows, P is work.

cards.title: Voltage — electrical pressure
body: Higher voltage = stronger push on electrons.
Examples: 120V receptacle, 240V dryer, 480V motor.
cards.remember: Voltage is the push — not the flow itself.

cards.title: Current (amperage) — the flow
body: How many electrons pass per second.
Too much current on too small a wire = heat and fire risk.
cards.remember: Amps measure flow — respect wire ampacity.

cards.title: Resistance — opposition to flow
body: Smaller wire = more resistance = less current.
Bigger wire = less resistance = more current allowed.
Resistance creates heat, voltage drop, and power loss.
cards.remember: Heat is the enemy — size wire right.

cards.title: Ohm's Law
cards.remember: Cover the value you want in the V-I-R triangle.

cards.title: Power formulas
body: Example: 120V × 10A = 1,200 watts.
cards.remember: Volts × amps = watts. Always.

cards.title: Common appliance power (120V examples)
cards.remember: Big heat and motors draw the most power.

cards.title: DC vs AC
body: DC (direct current): flows one direction — batteries, solar, electronics.
AC (alternating current): reverses direction — US homes use 60 Hz AC.
AC is used in homes and industry because voltage is easy to transform.
cards.remember: Home = AC. Batteries = DC.

cards.title: How AC flows in your home
list: Utility transformer → service → meter → main panel
Electrons loop: panel → load → back through neutral
Hot to neutral ≈ 120V; hot to hot ≈ 240V; neutral to ground ≈ 0V
cards.remember: Neutral carries current back — ground is safety.

cards.title: Series vs parallel circuits
body: Series: one path only — if one bulb breaks, all go out (old Christmas lights).
Parallel: multiple paths — one breaks, others keep working.
Homes use parallel so each room works independently.
cards.remember: Homes = parallel. Series = one break kills all.

cards.title: Wire size vs resistance
body: Bigger wire = less resistance = more current safely.
Breaker must match wire — breaker protects wire, not the appliance.
cards.remember: Small wire + big breaker = fire.

cards.title: Single-phase theory (residential)
body: Usually 120/240V. Transformer center-tap gives neutral.
Hot → neutral = 120V. Hot → hot = 240V.
cards.remember: Two hots + neutral = 120V and 240V from one service.

cards.title: Three-phase theory
body: Used in commercial, industrial, and utilities.
More efficient, smoother motors, more power.
Three-phase power formula: P = 1.732 × V × I (1.732 = √3).
cards.remember: 1.732 shows up everywhere in 3-phase — learn it.

cards.title: Delta systems (know the danger)
body: Pure delta has no neutral — used for motors and industrial loads.
High-leg delta has one 208V leg (often orange) — never use for 120V loads by mistake.
cards.remember: Orange high-leg on delta — mark it and respect it.

cards.title: Magnetism & transformers (why it matters)
list: Current through wire creates a magnetic field
Coils strengthen magnetism — basis of transformers, motors, relays, contactors
Transformer: primary voltage in, secondary voltage out (step up or down)
cards.remember: Motors and transformers are cousins — both use magnetism.

cards.title: Motor theory basics
list: Inrush current on start can be 6–10× running amps
Overload protects motor; contactor switches power; VFD controls speed
cards.remember: Inrush is why motor breakers can be larger than wire size (Article 430).

cards.title: Series vs parallel (theory summary)
cards.remember: Parallel = safe, independent circuits at home.

cards.title: Short circuit vs open circuit
body: Short circuit: fault hot-to-neutral, hot-to-ground, or phase-to-phase — massive current, breaker trips fast.
Open circuit: broken path — no current flow (loose neutral, cut wire).
cards.remember: Short = trip fast. Open = dead load, weird voltages.

cards.title: Power factor, capacitors, harmonics (intro)
list: Power factor: efficiency measure — ideal is 1.0; low PF wastes power
Capacitors: store energy — motor start, PF correction
Inductors: oppose current change — transformers, motors, ballasts
Harmonics: dirty waveforms from VFDs, computers, LEDs — can overheat neutrals
cards.remember: You will hear these more in commercial/industrial later.

cards.title: Continuous load rule (125%)
body: Continuous load = runs 3+ hours.
Size at 125% for some calculations.
On a 20A circuit, max continuous load ≈ 16A (20 × 0.8).
cards.remember: Heaters and motors that run long — do not max out the breaker.

cards.title: Voltage drop theory
body: Longer wire = more resistance = voltage loss at the load.
Target: branch under 3%, total feeder under 5%.
Field formula: VD = (2 × K × I × D) ÷ CM — K = resistivity, D = distance, CM = wire area.
cards.remember: Long run? Upsize wire — cheaper than a callback.

cards.title: Key takeaways (9 rules from the poster)
list: Electricity is electron flow in a closed loop
Voltage pushes, current flows, resistance slows
AC changes direction; DC flows one way
Ground is safety; neutral returns current
Understand basics, work safely, keep learning
cards.remember: Electricity wants a source, a path, and a return.

cards.title: Final theory rule (info5)
body: If you understand voltage, current, resistance, power, grounding, and fault paths — you understand the foundation of the entire electrical trade.
Electricity always wants: (1) a source, (2) a path, (3) to return to source.
cards.remember: Master the foundation — the code builds on this.

topics.title: Safety First
topics.description: LOTO, PPE, and test before touch
topics.intro: Safety is not a chapter you skip. These rules keep you alive on day one.

cards.title: Treat every wire as hot
body: Never assume a circuit is off because someone said so or because a switch is down.
Verify with your meter every time before you touch.
cards.remember: If you did not test it, it is still live.

cards.title: Lockout / Tagout (LOTO)
body: Shut off power at the breaker or disconnect.
Lock it so nobody can turn it back on.
Tag it with your name and why it is locked.
Test with a meter to prove zero energy.
cards.remember: Test before touch — every job, every time.

cards.title: Live → Dead → Live
cards.body: Before checking a dead circuit: test your meter on a known live source, test the circuit, then test the live source again. This proves your meter and leads work.

cards.title: PPE you actually wear
cards.remember: PPE only works if it is on your body.

cards.title: Never work live (as a new apprentice)
body: Working energized is for trained, authorized people on specific tasks.
Your job is to de-energize, lock out, and verify.
cards.remember: Say: I am not comfortable working it hot.

cards.title: Panel doors and clearances
body: Keep panel doors closed when you are not working inside.
NEC 110.26: about 3 ft deep, 30 in wide, 6.5 ft tall working space — never block panels with storage.
cards.remember: Neat work and clear space keep everyone safe.

cards.title: Arc flash & ladder safety
list: 480V can kill instantly — respect arc flash labels and PPE
Never stand on the top cap of a ladder
Arc flash can exceed 35,000°F — gear matters above 240V
cards.remember: Respect ALL gear on anything 240V and up.

cards.title: Field rules — never forget
list: Neutral carries current; ground normally does not
cards.remember: Tight terminations matter — torque to spec.

topics.title: How Power Gets to a Building
topics.description: From the utility to your lights and appliances
topics.intro: Follow the path of electricity from the pole to the outlet. When you see the whole picture, troubleshooting gets easier.

cards.title: The big picture
body: Utility transformer sends power to the building.
Service drop or underground feed goes to the meter.
Meter measures usage, then power enters the main panel.
Branch circuits feed lights, outlets, and big appliances.

image.alt: Residential and commercial power flow diagrams
image.caption: Tap to zoom — residential (top) and commercial (bottom).

cards.remember: Power always has a path: in on the hots, back on the neutral, safety on the ground.

cards.title: Residential path step by step
list: Main breaker panel (neutral and ground bonded here only)
cards.remember: The main panel is the first disconnect — neutral and ground bond only here.

cards.title: What the main breaker does
body: It shuts off all power to the house in one move.
Individual breakers only shut off their circuit.
cards.remember: Main off = whole house dark.

cards.title: 120V vs 240V at home

rows: Dryer, range, water heater, large AC

cards.remember: Two hots plus neutral give you both 120V and 240V.

cards.title: Common home circuits
cards.remember: Breaker protects the wire — not the appliance.

cards.title: Commercial power flow (simple)
body: Utility sends 3-phase power to a meter section and main switchboard.
Distribution panels feed lighting, kitchen equipment, and HVAC.
Large motors often use 480V from an MCC (motor control center).
cards.remember: Commercial has more voltage flavors: 120, 208, 277, and 480.

cards.title: Fast food restaurant example
cards.remember: Follow the one-line in your head: utility → meter → main → panels → loads.

cards.title: Incoming power family tree
body: UTILITY → METER → DISCONNECT → MAIN PANEL → SUB-PANEL(S) → LOADS.
Every install branches from this — only wire count and voltage change.
cards.remember: Draw this line on a napkin — it never changes.

cards.title: Voltage & phase quick-ID
body: *High-leg delta: one leg is 208V — do not feed 120V loads wrong.
cards.remember: Know your system before you land a wire.

cards.title: Service types — overhead & underground
list: Overhead: drip-loop 12" below weatherhead; neutral taped white in panel
Torque: 35 in-lb (#2 Al), 50 in-lb (4/0 Al) — verify label
Backfill: 6" sand, warning tape 12" above — inspectors dig
cards.remember: Service entrance details fail inspections when rushed.

cards.title: Solar backfeed (residential)
list: Solar panels → inverter → AC disconnect → backfed breaker in main panel
Follow labeling and NEC 690 — coordinate with AHJ
cards.remember: Solar adds a second source — labeling matters.

cards.title: 3-way & 4-way switches (from info2)
body: 3-way: two locations control one light (stairs, halls) — travelers between switches.
4-way: goes between two 3-ways for three or more switch locations.
cards.remember: 3-way = two switches; 4-way = middle switch in a chain.

topics.title: Single-Phase vs Three-Phase & Colors
topics.intro: Know what system you are on and which color is which phase. Interviewers ask this a lot.

cards.title: Residential = single-phase
body: 120/240V single-phase for houses, apartments, and small shops.
Two hots (often black and red), one neutral (white), one ground (green or bare).
cards.remember: Home = single-phase 120/240V.

cards.title: Commercial often = three-phase
body: 120/208V wye — offices, restaurants, retail.
277/480V wye — warehouses, big HVAC, industrial lighting.
cards.remember: More phases = more power for big equipment.

cards.title: 120/208V wire colors — BRB
cards.remember: BRB = Black, Red, Blue.

cards.title: 277/480V wire colors — BOY
cards.remember: BOY = Brown, Orange, Yellow.

cards.title: Residential conductor colors
cards.remember: White is neutral — not a hot unless code allows a marked exception.

cards.title: Voltage cheat sheet
cards.remember: Read the nameplate — voltage tells the truth.

cards.title: Where each system is used
list: 277/480V wye: warehouses, industrial plants, large HVAC
cards.remember: Interviewers LOVE BRB and BOY — say them out loud.

cards.title: Transformers step voltage
body: 480V → 208V is a common commercial example.
Transformers use electromagnetic induction.
cards.remember: Big buildings often get 480V and step down for 120V outlets.

topics.title: Panels: Main vs Sub
topics.intro: The #1 apprentice mistake is bonding neutral and ground together in a subpanel. Learn the rule once and never forget it.

cards.title: Main service panel
list: Neutral and ground are bonded together here only
cards.remember: Main panel = bond neutral and ground.

cards.title: Subpanel rules
list: Fed from main with 4 wires: 2 hots, neutral, ground
Ground bar bonded to the enclosure
No bond screw between neutral and ground
cards.body: NEVER bond neutral and ground in a subpanel. You create parallel paths and ghost trips.

cards.title: Bonding cheat card
cards.remember: Main bonds. Sub floats neutral.

cards.title: Landing feeder wires — safe order
body: With power locked out: ground first, neutral second, hots last.
Keeps your tools away from live lugs while you work.
cards.remember: Ground → neutral → hots.

cards.title: Subpanel feed example
body: Garage, workshop, or EV subpanel needs L1, L2, neutral, and ground.
Neutrals land only on the isolated neutral bar.
Grounds only on the ground bar tied to the can.
cards.remember: Four wires in — neutrals and grounds separated in the sub.

cards.title: Disconnect switch & MCC (know the names)
body: Disconnect: local shutoff for HVAC, motors, equipment service.
MCC: industrial cabinet with starters, VFDs, breakers, overloads.
cards.remember: Disconnect = local off. MCC = motor neighborhood.

cards.title: Panel install steps (field manual)
list: Bond/float correctly (main = bond, sub = float neutral)
Label everything before spaghetti sets in
cards.remember: Label BEFORE you land wires.

cards.title: 30-second panel checklist
list: Open door one-handed, face turned away
Bonding screw: IN at service, OUT at sub
Neutrals and grounds on separate bars in subs
cards.remember: Subpanel neutrals touching grounds = ghost trips later.

cards.title: Subpanel feeder sizing quick-pick
cards.remember: Longer run? Upsize wire or parallel conduit.

cards.title: Grounding electrode system (GEC)
body: Cold-water bond within 5 ft of entrance. Two rods 6 ft apart if one rod > 25Ω.
cards.remember: Ground rods protect — size GEC to service.

cards.title: Before energizing — final checklist
list: Record baseline amps on each leg after energizing
cards.remember: First start — write down amps for future troubleshooting.

cards.title: Special scenarios
list: Detached garage: 4-wire feeder, float neutral, ground rod(s)
Mobile home: 200A meter-main combo; float neutral inside home panel
RTU: 480V 3-ph, local disconnect within sight, HMCP breaker for inrush
cards.remember: Shed/garage sub = 4 wires + rods — not a 3-wire feed.

topics.title: Wire Sizes & Breakers
topics.intro: The breaker protects the wire. Size the wire first, then pick the breaker.

cards.title: Memory trick: 14-20-30-50-65-100
cards.remember: Breaker size must not exceed wire ampacity.

cards.title: Why this matters
body: If the breaker is too big for the wire, the wire can overheat before the breaker trips.
That is a fire risk. Never use whatever wire is on the truck without checking.
cards.remember: Breaker protects wire — not the toaster.

cards.title: Common wire types
cards.remember: Wire type must match where and how it is installed.

cards.title: Voltage drop (simple rule)
body: Long runs lose voltage. Keep branch drop under about 3%, total under about 5%.
If a run is over 100 ft, consider going up one wire size — cheaper than a callback.
cards.remember: Long run? Bigger wire.

cards.title: Continuous loads
body: Loads that run 3+ hours (heat, some motors) may need 125% when sizing.
For interviews: know that a 20A breaker is not always 20A of load forever.
cards.remember: Volts × amps = watts. Size for load and duty.

cards.title: Box fill (why boxes matter)
body: Each box has a max number of wires based on volume.
Count conductors, clamps, and devices — too many wires fails inspection.
cards.remember: Stuffing a box is not neat — it is a fail.

cards.title: NEC wire sizing (75°C column — info3)
cards.remember: CRITICAL: Breaker protects the wire — NOT the appliance.

cards.title: Conduit fill — NEC & fist rule
body: 40% max fill for more than 2 conductors (NEC Chapter 9).
THHN is typical in conduit.
cards.remember: Overfill = pull burns, failed inspection, angry foreman.

cards.title: Derating conductors
body: More conductors in a raceway = more heat = reduced ampacity.
Very important in commercial — count before you pull.
cards.remember: Full conduit runs hot — derate ampacity.

cards.title: Box fill (NEC 314.16)
list: All grounds = 1 count; all clamps = 1 count
cards.remember: A device counts as two wires — do not stuff boxes.

cards.title: Advanced terms (info1)
cards.remember: Hear a new word on site — look it up same day.

topics.title: Multimeter Mastery
topics.intro: Your meter is the most important tool in your bag. Learn the ports, the settings, and where to put the probes.

cards.title: What a multimeter measures
list: Current (A) — in series or use a clamp meter

image.caption: Tap to zoom — probe placement A through L.

cards.remember: Wrong setting + live circuit = damaged meter or injury.

cards.title: Meter ports

rows: High current only — then move back

cards.remember: Black to COM. Red to VΩ unless you are doing high amps in series.

cards.title: NEVER do this
list: Measure voltage on the amp port
Measure resistance or continuity on a live circuit
Move leads while touching live parts
Hold metal probe tips with your fingers
cards.body: These mistakes blow meters and hurt people.

cards.title: Test a 120V outlet
body: Dial: AC voltage (V~). Black to neutral (wide slot), red to hot (narrow slot).
Expect about 110–125V. Hot to ground should also read ~120V.
Neutral to ground should be near 0V.
cards.remember: Hot-neutral ~120. Neutral-ground ~0.

cards.title: Troubleshooting trick: open neutral
body: If hot-to-neutral reads 0 but hot-to-ground reads 120V, you may have an open neutral.
This is a common real-world problem — not magic.
cards.remember: Weird voltages mean follow the path — do not guess.

cards.title: Test 240V
body: Hot 1 to hot 2: expect ~240V.
Each hot to ground: expect ~120V each.
cards.remember: 240V is hot-to-hot. Each leg is still 120V to ground.

cards.title: Continuity test
body: Power OFF. Dial to continuity. Beep = complete path.
Use on switches, fuses, and wire ends.
cards.remember: No beep = open (broken). Beep = good path.

cards.title: Current — use a clamp when you can
body: Series amperage requires breaking the circuit — apprentices often blow meters here.
Clamp meters read amps around a single conductor — safer and faster.
cards.remember: Clamp around one wire — not both hots together.

cards.title: Poster: multimeter section
cards.body: Zoom the field poster multimeter quadrant for dial symbols and safety reminders.
cards.remember: Live → dead → live before you trust any reading.

cards.title: Golden safety rules (never)
list: Trust power is off without testing
cards.body: Misuse can blow the meter, arc flash you, or destroy equipment.

cards.title: Basic meter settings
cards.remember: Right dial, right port — every time.

cards.title: Resistance test on heaters
body: Power OFF. Low ohms usually = good element.
OL = open (burned). 0Ω = shorted — investigate.
cards.remember: OL on a heater often means replace the element.

cards.title: Testing capacitors (HVAC)
body: DISCHARGE capacitor before touching — can hold charge after power is off.
cards.remember: Capacitors bite — discharge first.

cards.title: Good meters to buy
cards.remember: Buy once, cry once — cheap meters lie.

cards.title: Fast troubleshooting order
cards.remember: Work top-down — source to load.

topics.title: Conduit Bending (EMT)
topics.intro: Clean bends look professional and save rework. EMT is the most common commercial raceway.

cards.title: Common bends

rows: Go over a pipe or obstacle

cards.remember: Measure twice, bend once.

cards.title: Bender parts
cards.remember: Line up the arrow with your mark.

cards.title: Stub-up 90 with take-up
body: Measure stub height wanted. Subtract take-up. Mark conduit at that distance.
Align arrow with mark. Bend to 90°.
cards.remember: 24" stub on 1/2" EMT: 24 − 5 = mark at 19".

cards.title: Offset multipliers
body: Obstacle height × multiplier = distance between marks.
Example: 4" obstacle at 30° → 4 × 2 = 8" between marks.
cards.remember: 30° offset is the fast field favorite (×2).

cards.title: Common bending mistakes
list: Kinked EMT from poor foot pressure
cards.remember: Keep conduit straight in the bender plane.

cards.title: Support rules (typical)
body: Support within 3 ft of a box and about every 10 ft along the run (check NEC for your install).
Good work is level, parallel, and symmetrical.
cards.remember: Ugly bends get ripped out — take your time.

cards.title: Shrink on offsets
body: Offsets shorten conduit slightly — about ¼" per inch of offset at 30°.
cards.remember: Account for shrink on long offset runs.

cards.title: 3-point saddle
body: Used to go over pipe, conduit, or obstacles — most common field saddle.
cards.remember: Saddle = three bends, one obstacle.

cards.title: Commercial install rules
list: Support within 3 ft of box, ~every 10 ft
Clean bends matter — crooked work gets ripped out
cards.remember: Conduit work is visible art — take pride.

cards.title: Offset multipliers (info3 alternate)
body: Offset height ÷ multiplier = distance between marks.
cards.remember: 30° ×2 is the fast field favorite.

topics.title: NEC Essentials
topics.intro: You do not memorize the whole NEC. You learn where to look and the big rules interviewers expect.

cards.title: What the NEC is for
body: NFPA 70 — practical safeguarding of people and property.
It is not a design manual. It sets minimum safe rules.
cards.remember: Code = minimum safe. Good craftsmen go beyond sloppy minimums.

cards.title: Top articles to know by name
cards.remember: Article 250 is the safety superstar.

cards.title: Article 250 — grounding & bonding
list: Service neutral bonding only at main
cards.remember: Grounding connects to earth. Bonding connects metal to clear faults fast.

cards.title: Article 110 — working space
body: 110.26: about 3 ft deep, 30 in wide, 6.5 ft tall in front of panels.
110.14: torque terminations — use a torque screwdriver on lugs.
cards.remember: Never block a panel with boxes or shelves.

cards.title: Article 300 — wiring methods
list: Same circuit conductors in same raceway
6 inches minimum free conductor in boxes (300.14)
cards.remember: Protection from physical damage — nail plates on studs.

cards.title: How to answer code questions in an interview
body: Say you use the index and tables — speed finding matters.
Admit when you do not know, but know which article you would open.
cards.remember: Honest + safe beats pretending to know everything.

cards.title: Master these 10 NEC articles (info4)

rows: Practical safeguarding — not a design manual
Same circuit in same raceway; burial depths

cards.remember: Know article numbers — speed in the book wins.

cards.title: More articles from info5
cards.remember: Pool? Article 680. MC support? 330.30.

cards.title: Article 210 & 220 highlights
cards.remember: 210 is where receptacles and AFCI/GFCI live.

cards.title: Article 250.122 — EGC sizing
body: #12 circuit → #12 ground; #10 circuit → #10 ground; #8 circuit → #10 ground (memorize pattern in table).
cards.remember: Equipment grounding conductor size follows table 250.122.

cards.title: NEC navigation interview line
body: If I do not know the exact line, I use the index and thumb tabs to find it within 60 seconds.
Admit what you do not know — show how you would look it up.
cards.remember: Honest + resourceful beats fake expert.

topics.title: GFCI, AFCI, Devices & Switches
topics.intro: Know what device does what job, where it is required, and how to wire a receptacle and switch.

cards.title: GFCI — protects people
body: Trips on small leakage current (around 4–6 mA).
Required near water: baths, kitchens, garages, outdoors, and more per 210.8.
cards.remember: GFCI = shock protection near water.

cards.title: AFCI — protects against fire
body: Detects dangerous arcing.
Required in many dwelling living areas per 210.12.
cards.remember: AFCI = arc / fire protection in living spaces.

cards.title: Device wiring colors
cards.remember: Brass hot, silver neutral, green ground.

cards.title: Pigtail neutrals
body: Do not depend on the device to carry neutral downstream.
Pigtail neutrals in the box — pros do it every time.
cards.remember: Pigtail the neutral — do not chain through the device.

cards.title: 3-way switches
body: Two switches control one light from two places (stairs, halls).
Uses traveler wires between 3-ways plus line and load.
cards.remember: 3-way = two locations, one light.

cards.title: 4-way switches
body: Goes between two 3-ways when you need three or more switch locations.
cards.remember: 4-way = middle switch between two 3-ways.

cards.title: Receptacle spacing (residential basics)
body: 210.52: walls — no point more than 6 ft from a receptacle (12 ft max apart).
Countertops: within 2 ft of edge; gaps matter on islands too.
cards.remember: 6 ft rule on walls — learn it before layout.

cards.title: Switch wiring (field manual)
cards.remember: Line on common — travelers only on 3-way/4-way.

cards.title: MWBC & 2-pole breakers
body: 2-pole breakers for 240V loads and multi-wire branch circuits.
Motor HMCP: high magnetic trip for motor inrush.
cards.remember: Two poles tied together = both legs off together.

cards.title: Countertop receptacle rule (detail)
body: Above counter within 2 ft of edge.
No point along counter more than 24 inches without a receptacle on islands/peninsulas.
cards.remember: Kitchen and bath layouts — measure before drywall.

topics.title: Commercial & Industrial Intro
topics.description: Motors, MCCs, PLCs, and bigger voltage
topics.intro: After residential basics, commercial and industrial work adds 3-phase gear and controls. Learn the words first.

cards.title: Commercial wiring methods
cards.remember: EMT indoors, MC branches, rigid where toughness matters.

cards.title: T-bar ceiling work
body: You will pull MC, EMT, lights, and often data in grid ceilings.
Keep runs parallel and supported — GCs and inspectors notice.
cards.remember: Ceiling work = neat parallel runs.

cards.title: Motors — basics
list: Overload sized to FLA on nameplate
Motor breakers can be larger than wire for inrush (Article 430)
cards.remember: Read the motor nameplate — FLA is your friend.

cards.title: VFD and PLC (know the names)
body: VFD: variable frequency drive — controls motor speed. Very common.
PLC: programmable logic controller — automation brain for conveyors, pumps, plants.
cards.remember: Controls and automation pay well later — learn terms now.

cards.title: Pull boxes
body: Needed when conduit runs are too long or have too many bends.
Pulling without boxes burns time and wire.
cards.remember: Too many bends? Add a pull box.

cards.title: Arc flash respect
cards.body: 480V systems can kill instantly. Wear required PPE, respect labels, and never work outside your training.

cards.title: Motor controls (info3)
list: Relay vs contactor: relay = small control; contactor = motor power
cards.remember: Follow schematic control path — voltage left to right.

cards.title: Ballasts & LED drivers
list: Never mix drivers and LED load types
cards.remember: Wrong driver = early failure or fire.

cards.title: PLC & automation
body: PLC = programmable logic controller — industrial automation computer.
Controls pumps, conveyors, water plants, factories.
Huge earning potential in controls and automation.
cards.remember: Learn motor basics first — PLC builds on that.

cards.title: Commercial panels (info2)
cards.remember: Follow the one-line: MSB → panels → loads.

cards.title: Troubleshooting — nothing works
cards.remember: 4 moves before you start swapping parts randomly.

cards.title: Motor won't start
cards.remember: Control circuit first — then power circuit.

topics.title: Tools & Jobsite Habits
topics.description: What to carry and how to act
topics.intro: Interviewers care more about reliability and hustle than genius-level code memory.

cards.title: First-year tools to own
cards.remember: Show up with tools — borrow less, learn more.

cards.title: First-year rules — ALWAYS
list: Ask questions once, then do the work
cards.remember: Hustle and cleanup get you invited back.

cards.title: First-year rules — NEVER
list: Work live without training and permission
cards.remember: Honesty about what you do not know builds trust.

cards.title: Daily 2-minute drill
list: Photo anything weird for the foreman
cards.remember: Plan like rocket science, work smooth, test like you are paranoid.

cards.title: Magic phrases that save you
list: I need to verify that with the foreman.
Can you show me on the print?
I am not comfortable working it hot.
cards.remember: Nobody got fired for being too safe.

cards.title: Print marking system
cards.remember: Photo every redline — that is your as-built backup.

cards.title: Field manual fundamentals
list: Voltage does not lie — verify with meter
Never assume a neutral is safe
cards.remember: Seven habits of a pro — not optional.

cards.title: Tool pouch must-haves (survival guide)
list: 11-in-1 in your hand, not buried in pouch
Tick tester — check on known hot every morning
Mini level — crooked panels = rookie label
Sharpie and white tape — label while you work
cards.remember: Phone eats first — photo before disconnect.

cards.title: Common jobsite fails
list: Forgot MC ring — pull whole run
Drywall buried your box — photo and flag
Duct tape on PVC instead of proper glue
Wire nuts left in panel — rookie billboard
cards.remember: Slow down on materials — redo costs more.

cards.title: Print reading basics
cards.remember: Prints lie sometimes — meter tells truth.

cards.title: Fire alarm & low voltage
body: Fire alarm: electrician provides power and conduit.
Low voltage: stub boxes, conduit, labeling — coordinate with LV trades.
cards.remember: You power FA — FA company wires devices.

cards.title: Change orders & as-builts
list: GC says just install it — stop, get clarification
Document change orders before extra work
Redline prints daily — your as-built when sets get lost
cards.remember: If it is not on paper, it did not happen.

cards.title: Engineer red flags
cards.remember: Protect yourself — ask before you burn money.

topics.title: Interview & Career
topics.intro: You are not expected to know everything. You are expected to be safe, reliable, and eager to learn.

cards.title: What interviewers actually want
cards.remember: Attitude beats memorizing the whole NEC.

cards.title: Why electrician?
body: I like working with my hands, solving problems, and learning a skilled trade with long-term growth.
cards.remember: Keep answers short and real.

cards.title: What do you know already?
body: I study NEC basics — grounding and bonding, residential vs commercial, conduit, and safety. I verify every circuit before I touch it.
cards.remember: Mention test-before-touch — it marks you as safe.

cards.title: Low-voltage background (if that is you)
body: I have pulled cable, terminated, followed paths, and kept work neat for testing.
I am ready to apply that discipline to higher voltage and learn the NEC on the job.
cards.remember: Neat low-voltage work proves you can learn high-voltage right.

cards.title: Five concepts that sound smart
cards.remember: Five solid topics beat fifty vague ones.

cards.title: Fastest way to earn respect
cards.remember: Respect on site is earned with actions, not talk.

cards.title: High-value skills for years 2–4
cards.remember: Specialize later — master safety and basics first.

cards.title: Best interview answers (verbatim from info1)
body: Why electrician? — I like working with my hands, solving problems, and learning a skilled trade with long-term growth.
What do you know? — NEC basics, grounding/bonding, residential vs commercial, conduit, and safety.
Most important? — Safety and doing quality work correctly the first time.
cards.remember: Short, honest, safety-first.

cards.title: Interview phrases (info4)
list: I always work safely — I treat every wire as hot until I prove it is not.
I am comfortable pulling cable and keeping work neat.
I study NEC — Articles 250, 300, and 310 right now.
I would rather ask twice than make a mistake once.
cards.remember: Sprinkle these naturally — do not recite like a robot.

cards.title: 30-minute daily study plan (info4)

rows: Find examples in a panel photo
Names: EMT, PVC, rigid; watch stub 90
Name every tool in your bag

cards.remember: 30 minutes daily beats cramming.

cards.title: High-value skills years 2–4 (full list)
cards.remember: Pick one specialty later — master basics first.

cards.title: Career paths the trade offers
cards.remember: The trade is a ladder — first rung is showing up safe.

cards.title: Master electrician level (long-term)
cards.remember: You do not need this day one — know it exists.

cards.title: What’s most important in interview
body: Not genius-level code knowledge.
They want reliability, safety, work ethic, willingness to learn, showing up every day.
cards.remember: Attitude and safety beat memorizing the whole NEC.

topics.title: Quick Reference Tables
topics.intro: Bookmark this topic in your head for field days — wire, colors, and formulas in one place.

cards.title: Ohm's law & power
cards.remember: Volts push, amps flow, ohms resist.

cards.title: Grounding vs bonding

rows: Connection to earth — stabilize voltage, surges
Metal connected together — fault path to trip breaker

cards.remember: They are not the same thing — interviewers trap you here.

cards.title: Conduit fill — fist rule (#12 THHN)
cards.remember: Overfill = hard pull, hot wire, failed inspection.

cards.title: Multimeter readings (AC)
cards.remember: Voltage tells the truth. Prints lie sometimes.

cards.title: Abbreviations
cards.remember: Learn the language — it speeds up every conversation on site.

cards.title: Apprentice quick memory — three worlds
cards.remember: Know which world you are in before you touch anything.

cards.title: Master's bottom line
body: Power does not care how smart you sound.
It cares how tight, how big, and how right your neutrals and grounds are.
cards.remember: Test before touch. Every time.

cards.title: Troubleshooting method (7 steps)
cards.remember: Work backwards from load to source.

cards.title: kVA calculation
body: kVA = VA ÷ 1000
cards.remember: Transformers and services often rated in kVA.

cards.title: Torque values (common)
cards.remember: Torque screwdriver — inspectors may watch.

cards.title: Three load types (theory)
list: Resistive: heat, incandescent — steady on breakers
cards.remember: Motors are hardest on breakers — Article 430.

cards.title: NEC 110.26 working space
cards.remember: Never block a panel with storage.

cards.title: Relays vs contactors
body: Relay: small control circuits.
Contactor: large power — usually motors.
cards.remember: Coil voltage on contactor must match control circuit.

cards.title: Master memory rules (info5)
cards.remember: Voltage tells the truth. Prints lie. Your meter is king.
